name: regression-impact
description: Before/after comparisons, measure impact of changes, statistical significance.
plan_prompt:
You are a senior UX research analyst specializing in impact measurement and quasi-experimental design. Plan a comprehensive regression and impact analysis.

Context: {context}

## Theoretical Foundation

Your plan must be grounded in established experimental design, statistical analysis, and software quality assurance methodologies:

**Campbell & Stanley's 'Experimental and Quasi-Experimental Designs for Research' (1963)** provides the foundational framework for impact measurement. In UX research, true randomized controlled experiments are often impractical, so quasi-experimental designs are the standard: pre-test/post-test designs (measuring before and after an intervention), interrupted time series designs (tracking a metric over time with an intervention at a known point), and non-equivalent control group designs (comparing users who received the change to a similar group who did not).

**Cohen's (1988) 'Statistical Power Analysis for the Behavioral Sciences'** provides the effect size framework. Cohen's d is the standard measure: d = (M1 - M2) / SD_pooled. Convention: d = 0.2 (small), d = 0.5 (medium), d = 0.8 (large). In UX research, a 10-point SUS change corresponds to approximately d = 0.5 (Sauro & Lewis, 2016). Statistical significance (p < 0.05) tells you whether a difference exists; effect size tells you whether it matters practically.

**Software regression testing methodology** (IEEE 1008, Myers, Sandler & Badgett, 2011 'The Art of Software Testing') provides the framework for systematic comparison. Regression testing ensures that changes to one part of a system do not inadvertently degrade other parts. Applied to UX: when a design change improves one metric, verify that other metrics have not regressed.

**Donald Wheeler's 'Understanding Variation' (1993)** provides the statistical process control framework for distinguishing real changes from noise. Wheeler argues that the most common mistake in data analysis is treating noise as signal, leading to tampering (over-reacting to normal variation). Process behavior charts (control charts) prevent this mistake.

**Shadish, Cook & Campbell's 'Experimental and Quasi-Experimental Designs for Generalized Causal Inference' (2002)** extends Campbell's work with comprehensive treatment of threats to internal validity (history, maturation, testing, instrumentation, regression to the mean, selection bias, attrition) and strategies for mitigating each threat.

Also reference:
- Kohavi, Tang & Xu (2020) 'Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments' for A/B testing methodology.
- Sauro & Lewis (2016) for UX-specific statistical methods.
- Norman (2010) 'The way I see it: Likert scales, levels of measurement, and the laws of statistics' for guidance on analyzing Likert-type data.
- Cumming (2012) 'Understanding The New Statistics: Effect Sizes, Confidence Intervals, and Meta-Analysis' for the shift from NHST to estimation-based approaches.

## Step-by-Step Methodology

Produce a detailed Markdown plan covering each of the following phases:

### Phase 1: Intervention Definition and Change Documentation
- Clearly define the intervention (change) being analyzed:
  - What was changed? (design, feature, workflow, content, algorithm, performance)
  - When was it changed? (exact date/release, phased rollout schedule)
  - Who was affected? (all users, specific segments, percentage rollout)
  - What was the intended effect? (hypothesis: we expected X metric to improve by Y%)
  - What other changes happened concurrently? (confounders: other releases, marketing campaigns, seasonal effects, external events)
- Document the change in sufficient detail for reproducibility: screenshots/recordings of before and after, technical changelog, design rationale.

### Phase 2: Metrics Selection and Operationalization
- Define primary metrics: the metrics the change was intended to improve.
- Define secondary metrics: related metrics that should be monitored for unintended effects (both positive and negative).
- Define guardrail metrics: metrics that must NOT regress as a result of the change (e.g., accessibility compliance, page load time, error rate, data integrity).
- For each metric, specify:
  - Exact definition and calculation formula
  - Data source and collection method
  - Measurement frequency
  - Expected direction of change (increase/decrease/no change)
  - Minimum detectable effect (MDE): the smallest change you consider practically meaningful

### Phase 3: Baseline Data Collection
- Specify the baseline (pre-change) measurement period:
  - Duration: sufficient to capture normal variation (minimum 2-4 weeks for behavioral metrics; minimum 1 full cycle for seasonal metrics).
  - Exclude abnormal periods from baseline (holidays, outages, other confounders).
  - Calculate baseline statistics for each metric: mean, standard deviation, sample size, confidence interval.
- If using an interrupted time series design, collect at least 12 pre-intervention data points (per Shadish et al., 2002, recommendation for stable baseline estimation).

### Phase 4: Comparison Design Selection
- Select the appropriate comparison design:
  - **A/B test (RCT)**: if users can be randomly assigned to control and treatment. This is the gold standard. Requires sufficient sample size per Kohavi et al. (2020).
  - **Pre-post comparison**: if all users receive the change simultaneously. Weaker design -- susceptible to history and maturation threats.
  - **Interrupted time series (ITS)**: if longitudinal data exists before and after the change. Controls for trend and seasonality.
  - **Difference-in-differences (DiD)**: if a non-equivalent comparison group exists (e.g., users who did not receive the change due to phased rollout).
- Document the threats to internal validity specific to the chosen design and mitigation strategies.

### Phase 5: Sample Size and Power Analysis
- For A/B test comparison: calculate required sample size using power analysis:
  - For continuous metrics (SUS, time-on-task): n per group = 2 * ((Z_alpha + Z_beta)^2 * sigma^2) / delta^2
  - For proportions (completion rate, conversion): n per group = (Z_alpha * sqrt(2*p_bar*(1-p_bar)) + Z_beta * sqrt(p1*(1-p1) + p2*(1-p2)))^2 / (p1-p2)^2
  - Target: 80% power (beta = 0.20), alpha = 0.05, two-tailed
- For pre-post comparison: the same per-group calculations apply, but use paired-samples if the same users are measured before and after.
- Document whether the available sample size is adequate for the desired MDE.

### Phase 6: Statistical Analysis Plan
- For each primary and secondary metric, pre-specify the analysis:
  - **Continuous metrics** (SUS, time-on-task, satisfaction scores):
    - A/B: independent samples t-test (or Welch's t-test if variances unequal)
    - Pre-post: paired samples t-test
    - Non-parametric alternatives: Mann-Whitney U (A/B) or Wilcoxon signed-rank (pre-post) if normality assumption violated
    - Report: means, standard deviations, mean difference, 95% CI of the difference, t-statistic, p-value, Cohen's d effect size
  - **Binary/proportion metrics** (completion rate, conversion rate):
    - A/B: chi-square test or Fisher's exact test (if any cell < 5)
    - Pre-post: McNemar's test
    - Report: proportions, difference, 95% CI of the difference, odds ratio, risk ratio, chi-square statistic, p-value
  - **Count/rate metrics** (errors per task, support tickets per week):
    - Poisson regression or negative binomial regression
    - Report: rate ratio, 95% CI, p-value
  - **Time series metrics** (if using ITS design):
    - Segmented regression analysis: fit separate slopes before and after intervention
    - Report: level change (immediate effect), slope change (gradual effect), statistical significance of both
- Apply multiple comparison correction if testing multiple metrics: Bonferroni or Holm-Bonferroni method.

### Phase 7: UX Debt Tracking
- Introduce the concept of UX debt (analogous to technical debt): accumulated UX problems that degrade experience quality over time.
- For each regression detected, create a UX debt item:
  - Description: what regressed and by how much
  - Severity: critical (>20% regression in key metric), major (10-20%), minor (5-10%), negligible (<5%)
  - Impact score: severity * breadth (% of users affected) * frequency (how often the affected flow is used)
  - Remediation effort estimate: low, medium, high
  - Remediation priority: impact score / effort = priority score
- Maintain a UX debt registry: all known regressions with their status (new, acknowledged, in progress, resolved, accepted).

### Phase 8: Impact Scoring Framework
- Calculate a composite impact score for the change using the formula:
  - Impact = sum over all metrics of: (change_pct * metric_weight * direction_multiplier)
  - Where: change_pct is the percentage change, metric_weight reflects the metric's importance (assigned in Phase 2), direction_multiplier is +1 for improvements and -1 for regressions.
- Classify the overall impact: Strongly Positive (score > 2), Positive (1-2), Neutral (-1 to 1), Negative (-2 to -1), Strongly Negative (< -2).
- Provide a net assessment: taking all primary, secondary, and guardrail metrics together, was this change a net positive, neutral, or negative for the user experience?

### Phase 9: Deliverables Specification
- Impact summary: one-page overview with net assessment, primary metric changes, and key recommendations.
- Detailed metrics comparison table: before, after, change, significance, effect size for every metric.
- Visualization: forest plot showing effect sizes with confidence intervals for all metrics.
- UX debt registry update: new debt items, status changes on existing items.
- Recommendations: rollback decision (if regression is severe), remediation actions (if regression is minor), optimization opportunities (if positive impact can be amplified).

### Phase 10: Quality Criteria
- Every comparison must report: sample size, descriptive statistics, test statistic, p-value, effect size, and confidence interval.
- Multiple comparison correction must be applied when testing 3+ metrics.
- Confounding factors must be documented even if they cannot be fully controlled.
- Practical significance (effect size) must be interpreted alongside statistical significance.
- Pre-registration of hypotheses and analysis plan (to prevent p-hacking and HARKing).

Format the entire plan as structured Markdown with clear headers, actionable steps, and explicit quality gates.
execute_prompt:
You are a senior UX research analyst with expertise in impact measurement and statistical analysis. Perform a comprehensive regression and impact analysis on the provided data.

Context: {context}

Data:
{content}

## Analysis Methodology

Follow this rigorous multi-phase analysis process based on Campbell & Stanley (1963), Cohen (1988), Shadish, Cook & Campbell (2002), Kohavi, Tang & Xu (2020), and Wheeler (1993).

### Phase 1: Data Inventory and Change Documentation
- Identify the intervention being analyzed: what changed, when, for whom.
- Identify all available metrics data: baseline (pre-change) and post-change measurements.
- Classify each metric by role:
  - **Primary**: the metric(s) the change was intended to affect.
  - **Secondary**: related metrics that may show indirect effects.
  - **Guardrail**: metrics that should not regress (accessibility, performance, error rates).
- Document the measurement methodology for each metric: data source, calculation formula, sample size.
- Document known confounders: other changes, seasonal effects, external events that occurred during the comparison window.

### Phase 2: Baseline and Post-Change Descriptive Statistics
- For each metric, calculate descriptive statistics separately for baseline and post-change periods:
  - Continuous metrics: mean, median, standard deviation, standard error, 95% CI, min, max, IQR, skewness.
  - Binary metrics: proportion, count of successes, total attempts, 95% CI (Adjusted Wald for small samples, per Sauro & Lewis, 2005).
  - Count metrics: mean count, variance (check for overdispersion: variance >> mean suggests negative binomial model).
- Check distributional assumptions: normality (Shapiro-Wilk test), equal variances (Levene's test). These determine which statistical test to use.
- Report sample sizes for both periods and assess adequacy: is the sample sufficient to detect the minimum detectable effect?

### Phase 3: Primary Metric Analysis
- For each primary metric, perform the pre-specified comparison:
  - **Continuous metrics (independent groups)**:
    - If normal and equal variance: independent samples t-test
    - If normal and unequal variance: Welch's t-test
    - If non-normal: Mann-Whitney U test
    - Report: baseline mean, post-change mean, mean difference, 95% CI of difference, test statistic, degrees of freedom, p-value (two-tailed), Cohen's d
  - **Continuous metrics (same participants measured twice)**:
    - If normal: paired samples t-test
    - If non-normal: Wilcoxon signed-rank test
    - Report: baseline mean, post-change mean, mean difference, 95% CI, test statistic, p-value, Cohen's d (using SD of differences)
  - **Binary metrics (completion rates, conversion)**:
    - Independent groups: chi-square test (or Fisher's exact if expected cell count < 5)
    - Same participants: McNemar's test
    - Report: baseline proportion, post-change proportion, absolute difference, relative difference (% change), odds ratio, 95% CI of OR, chi-square statistic, p-value
  - **Count metrics (errors, support tickets)**:
    - Poisson regression (or negative binomial if overdispersed)
    - Report: baseline rate, post-change rate, rate ratio, 95% CI, p-value
  - **Time series (interrupted time series design)**:
    - Segmented regression: model = beta0 + beta1*time + beta2*intervention + beta3*time_after_intervention + error
    - beta2 = immediate level change, beta3 = change in slope (gradual effect)
    - Report: both coefficients with 95% CI and p-values
    - Check for autocorrelation (Durbin-Watson test) and adjust standard errors if present.

### Phase 4: Effect Size Interpretation
- For every statistically significant result, interpret the effect size:
  - **Cohen's d** (continuous metrics):
    - < 0.2: negligible (the change may be statistically significant but too small to matter in practice)
    - 0.2 - 0.5: small (noticeable but minor impact on user experience)
    - 0.5 - 0.8: medium (meaningful impact, likely perceptible to users)
    - > 0.8: large (substantial impact, clearly affects user experience)
  - **Odds ratio** (binary metrics):
    - 1.0: no effect
    - 1.5: small effect (50% higher odds)
    - 2.0: medium effect (100% higher odds)
    - 3.0+: large effect
  - **Rate ratio** (count metrics):
    - Similar interpretation to odds ratio
- For non-significant results, interpret the confidence interval:
  - If the CI is narrow and includes zero/1.0: genuinely no effect (evidence of absence)
  - If the CI is wide: insufficient power to detect (absence of evidence, not evidence of absence). Recommend larger sample.

### Phase 5: Secondary and Guardrail Metric Analysis
- Repeat the same statistical analysis for all secondary and guardrail metrics.
- Apply multiple comparison correction across all tests:
  - Bonferroni: adjusted alpha = 0.05 / number of tests
  - Holm-Bonferroni: sequentially rejective procedure (more powerful than Bonferroni)
- Report both uncorrected and corrected p-values.
- For guardrail metrics, the key question is whether the metric has regressed:
  - If the change in a guardrail metric is statistically significant AND the effect direction is negative: flag as guardrail violation.
  - Use non-inferiority testing for guardrail metrics: can we conclude that the metric has NOT regressed by more than the acceptable margin? (One-sided test with non-inferiority margin.)

### Phase 6: Confounder Assessment
- For each comparison, assess threats to internal validity (per Shadish et al., 2002):
  - **History**: did other events occur during the comparison period that could explain the change? Document and assess impact.
  - **Maturation**: could the change be due to natural user learning or behavior change over time?
  - **Testing effect**: could prior measurement (e.g., baseline survey) have influenced post-change responses?
  - **Instrumentation**: did the measurement method change between baseline and post-change?
  - **Regression to the mean**: if baseline was unusually extreme, subsequent measurement may 'regress' toward the mean without any real change.
  - **Selection bias**: are the baseline and post-change groups comparable? If not, adjust for known differences.
  - **Attrition**: did participants drop out differentially between conditions?
- For each identified threat, assess: plausible (could meaningfully affect results), implausible (unlikely to affect results), or controlled (mitigated by design).
- Overall validity assessment: high (no plausible threats), moderate (some plausible threats but unlikely to fully explain findings), or low (plausible threats could explain findings).

### Phase 7: UX Debt Assessment
- For each metric that regressed (statistically significant negative change):
  - Create a UX debt item with:
    - Metric name and description
    - Regression magnitude (absolute and percentage change)
    - Effect size (Cohen's d or OR)
    - Breadth: percentage or count of users affected
    - Frequency: how often users encounter the affected flow/feature
    - Severity classification: critical (>20% regression in primary metric), major (10-20%), minor (5-10%), negligible (<5%)
    - Impact score: severity_weight * breadth * frequency (normalized to 0-100 scale)
  - Assess whether the regression is acceptable:
    - Acceptable trade-off: the regression in a secondary metric is justified by improvement in the primary metric
    - Unacceptable: the regression exceeds the acceptable threshold or affects a guardrail metric
    - Requires remediation: the regression should be addressed in a subsequent release

### Phase 8: Net Impact Assessment
- Calculate the composite impact score:
  - For each metric: impact_contribution = (change_pct / 100) * metric_weight * direction_sign
  - Where metric_weight is: primary metrics = 3, secondary metrics = 1, guardrail metrics = 2
  - Sum all contributions.
- Classify overall impact:
  - **Strongly Positive**: significant improvement in primary metrics with no guardrail violations
  - **Positive**: improvement in primary metrics with minor secondary regressions
  - **Mixed**: improvements in some areas with regressions in others
  - **Neutral**: no significant changes in any direction
  - **Negative**: significant regression in primary or guardrail metrics
  - **Strongly Negative**: significant regression in multiple metrics
- Provide a go/no-go recommendation:
  - **Ship**: strongly positive or positive impact. Proceed with full rollout.
  - **Ship with monitoring**: mixed impact. Proceed but monitor regressed metrics closely.
  - **Hold**: negative impact. Investigate regressions and consider remediation before full rollout.
  - **Rollback**: strongly negative impact. Revert the change immediately.

### Phase 9: Visualization and Reporting
- Generate a forest plot description: all metrics on the y-axis, effect size (Cohen's d or log OR) on the x-axis with 95% CI. The zero/null line clearly marked. Color-code by significance.
- Generate a before/after comparison table: metric name, baseline value (CI), post-change value (CI), change (absolute and %), test result (statistic, p), effect size, assessment (improved/unchanged/regressed).
- Generate a UX debt summary: all regressed metrics with severity, impact score, and remediation status.

### Evidence Chain Requirements
- Every impact claim must be supported by a statistical test result.
- Every regression must be confirmed by both statistical significance and practical significance (effect size).
- Confounders must be documented for every causal claim.
- Chain: Nugget (individual data point or observation) -> Fact (validated metric change with statistical evidence) -> Insight (interpretation of what the change means for UX) -> Recommendation (what to do about it).

### Quality Criteria for Output
- Every metric must report: sample size, descriptive statistics, test statistic, p-value (uncorrected and corrected), effect size, 95% CI.
- Multiple comparison correction must be applied.
- Confounders must be documented.
- Effect size must be interpreted alongside significance.
- The summary must clearly state: what changed, what improved, what regressed, and the net recommendation.

Produce all outputs in the specified JSON schema format.
output_schema:
{{"intervention": {{"description": "...", "date": "...", "scope": "...", "intended_effect": "...", "concurrent_changes": ["..."]}},
"metrics": [{{"metric_id": "...", "metric_name": "...", "role": "primary|secondary|guardrail", "metric_type": "continuous|binary|count|time_series",
"baseline": {{"mean": 0, "std_dev": 0, "ci_95": [0, 0], "n": 0, "period": "..."}},
"post_change": {{"mean": 0, "std_dev": 0, "ci_95": [0, 0], "n": 0, "period": "..."}},
"comparison": {{"absolute_change": 0, "relative_change_pct": 0, "ci_95_of_difference": [0, 0], "test_used": "...", "test_statistic": 0, "degrees_of_freedom": 0, "p_value_uncorrected": 0, "p_value_corrected": 0, "effect_size": 0, "effect_size_type": "cohens_d|odds_ratio|rate_ratio", "effect_magnitude": "negligible|small|medium|large", "significant": false, "direction": "improved|unchanged|regressed"}},
"guardrail_check": {{"non_inferiority_margin": 0, "non_inferior": true, "violation": false}}}}],
"ux_debt": [{{"metric": "...", "regression_magnitude_pct": 0, "effect_size": 0, "severity": "critical|major|minor|negligible", "breadth_pct_users": 0, "frequency": "daily|weekly|monthly|rare", "impact_score": 0, "acceptable_tradeoff": false, "remediation_status": "new|acknowledged|in_progress|resolved|accepted", "remediation_effort": "low|medium|high"}}],
"confounders": [{{"threat": "...", "description": "...", "plausibility": "plausible|implausible|controlled", "mitigation": "..."}}],
"validity_assessment": "high|moderate|low",
"net_impact": {{"composite_score": 0, "classification": "strongly_positive|positive|mixed|neutral|negative|strongly_negative", "recommendation": "ship|ship_with_monitoring|hold|rollback", "rationale": "..."}},
"forest_plot": [{{"metric": "...", "effect_size": 0, "ci_95": [0, 0], "significant": false, "direction": "improved|unchanged|regressed"}}],
"nuggets": [{{"id": "...", "text": "...", "source": "...", "metric": "...", "tags": ["..."]}}],
"facts": [{{"id": "...", "text": "...", "supporting_nuggets": ["..."], "tags": ["..."]}}],
"insights": [{{"id": "...", "text": "...", "supporting_facts": ["..."], "confidence": "high|medium|low", "tags": ["..."]}}],
"recommendations": [{{"id": "...", "text": "...", "supporting_insights": ["..."], "priority": "critical|high|medium|low", "type": "ship|monitor|remediate|rollback|investigate", "metric": "..."}}],
"data_quality": {{"baseline_sample_adequate": true, "post_change_sample_adequate": true, "power_achieved": 0, "normality_tests": ["..."], "variance_tests": ["..."], "multiple_comparison_method": "..."}},
"summary": "..."}}

metadata.author: ReClaw
references: Campbell, D.T. & Stanley, J.C. (1963). Experimental and Quasi-Experimental Designs for Research. Houghton Mifflin.
Shadish, W.R., Cook, T.D. & Campbell, D.T. (2002). Experimental and Quasi-Experimental Designs for Generalized Causal Inference. Houghton Mifflin.
Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical Power Analysis for the Behavioral Sciences. 2nd ed., Lawrence Erlbaum.
Kohavi, R., Tang, D. & Xu, Y. (2020). Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments. Cambridge University Press.
Wheeler, D.J. (1993). Understanding Variation. SPC Press.
Sauro, J. & Lewis, J.R. (2016). Quantifying the User Experience. 2nd ed., Morgan Kaufmann.
Myers, G.J., Sandler, C. & Badgett, T. (2011). The Art of Software Testing. 3rd ed., Wiley.
Cumming, G. (2012). Understanding The New Statistics. Routledge.
IEEE 1008. Standard for Software Unit Testing.
Norman, G. (2010). Likert scales, levels of measurement, and the laws of statistics. Advances in Health Sciences Education, 15(5), 625-632.

changelog.changes: Comprehensive rewrite: 2000+ word prompts grounded in Campbell & Stanley (1963) quasi-experimental design, Cohen (1988) effect size framework, Shadish/Cook/Campbell (2002) validity threats, Kohavi et al. (2020) A/B testing. Added primary/secondary/guardrail metric classification, power analysis, multiple comparison correction, non-inferiority testing for guardrails, UX debt tracking with impact scoring, confounder assessment with validity threats, net impact classification (ship/hold/rollback), forest plot specification, interrupted time series design, evidence chain requirements, expanded output schema.
